does: This work develops methods to improve how AI systems learn from human feedback and enhance human capacity to evaluate AI behavior. The authors focus on training AI systems to better incorporate human preferences and guidance, while simultaneously developing tools that enable humans to more effectively assess AI capabilities and safety properties. The core contribution centers on creating feedback loops where AI systems become increasingly responsive to human direction, with the ultimate aim of reaching a level of alignment where such systems can reliably assist in solving downstream alignment challenges.
connects: This research builds on a long line of work in learning from human feedback, including reinforcement learning from human preferences and techniques like RLHF that have become standard in language model training. It extends earlier efforts to address the challenge of scalable oversight, where human evaluators cannot directly assess all aspects of complex AI behavior, by focusing on bidirectional improvement: making AI better at interpretation of human intent while making humans more capable evaluators. The work responds to fundamental questions about iterated alignment raised in the AI safety community regarding whether sufficiently aligned systems could recursively help solve remaining alignment problems.
enables: Success in this direction could enable a powerful recursive alignment strategy where improved AI systems progressively assist humans in evaluating and aligning even more capable successor systems. This approach suggests a path toward handling the alignment tax of increasingly powerful models by having aligned systems themselves participate in oversight and alignment verification. The work points toward empirical testing of whether learning from human feedback can scale to address safety properties beyond obvious behavioral harms, and whether aligned systems can genuinely contribute novel insights to technical alignment research rather than merely executing human-directed evaluation.
